1: A structural note: The date formula places this oracle in January 587 BCE — the tenth year, tenth month, twelfth day, during the final siege of Jerusalem. The Christological backdrop: the nations surrounding Israel — Egypt, Tyre, Edom, Moab, Ammon — are all judged in Ezekiel 25–32, clearing the way for YHWH's restoration of his people. Every national judgment oracle in the prophets points toward the day when all powers hostile to YHWH's kingdom will be broken and Christ will reign over all (Revelation 11:15).
2: A structural note: 'Son of man, set your face against Pharaoh king of Egypt and prophesy against him.' The prophetic commissioning sets Ezekiel as YHWH's spokesperson against the world's greatest empire. Christ commissions his apostles with the same authority in reverse: not “prophesy against” but “proclaim the gospel to every nation” (Matthew 28:19). The apostolic mission is the positive counterpart to the prophetic-judgment commission, both proceeding from the divine throne.
3: A type: 'Behold I am against you, Pharaoh king of Egypt, the great dragon that lies in the midst of his streams, who says, My Nile is my own; I made it for myself.' The great dragon (tannin gadol) who claims self-creation is the recurring anti-type of every power that displaces YHWH. The dragon imagery runs from Genesis and Exodus through Ezekiel into Revelation's great red dragon (Revelation 12:3) and the beast from the sea (Revelation 13:1). Christ's victory over the dragon at the cross (Colossians 2:15; Revelation 12:11) is the eschatological counterpart: every earthly power that claims self-creation and self-sovereignty is judged by the one who actually created all things.
4: A pattern: 'And I will put hooks in your jaws and make the fish of your streams stick to your scales and I will draw you up out of the midst of your streams.' YHWH's sovereignty over the great dragon is expressed through fishing imagery — the hook, the net, the dragging out. God is never the one caught; the powers that oppose him are always the ones netted. This pattern culminates in Revelation: the beast and false prophet are captured and thrown into the lake of fire (Revelation 19:20). Christ's binding of the strong man (Matthew 12:29) is the inaugurated form of the final binding of the great dragon.
5: A pattern: 'And I will cast you out into the wilderness, you and all the fish of your streams; you shall fall on the open field and not be brought together or gathered. To the beasts of the earth and to the birds of the heavens I have given you as food.' The great dragon not just defeated but devoured — stripped of dignity and abandoned to scavengers. This is the pattern of Revelation 19:17-18: the great supper of God where the birds feast on the flesh of kings and captains. The humiliation of power is complete. Christ's resurrection is the reverse pattern: the one who was killed did not remain in the grave but was raised and glorified.
6: A type: 'Then all the inhabitants of Egypt shall know that I am YHWH, because they have been a staff of reed to the house of Israel.' Egypt as the broken reed (qaneh) is one of the OT's primary anti-types for false security. Isaiah 36:6 uses the same image: the Rabshakeh taunts Judah, “Behold you are relying on Egypt, that broken reed of a staff which will pierce the hand of any man who leans on it.” Christ is the true staff — “thy rod and thy staff they comfort me” (Psalms 23:4). Every broken reed is an invitation to find the one who will not break.
7: A pattern: 'When they grasped you with the hand, you broke and tore all their shoulders; and when they leaned on you, you broke and made all their loins to shake.' The broken reed pierces and tears — the thing Israel leaned on for strength became the source of injury. The pattern is inverted in the NT: Christ is the one who bears our burdens and gives rest to the weary (Matthew 11:28-30). The yoke he offers is easy and his burden is light — the complete antithesis of the Egypt-reed that punctures the hand of anyone who leans on it.
8: A pattern: 'Therefore thus says the Lord GOD: Behold I will bring a sword upon you and will cut off from you man and beast.' The sword of divine judgment is wielded by YHWH himself — Egypt's fall is not random military misfortune but divine verdict. This pattern points toward Christ as the one who comes with the sharp two-edged sword from his mouth (Revelation 1:16; Ezekiel 19:15). Every national judgment oracle in the prophets is a proleptic form of the final judgment that Christ will execute.
9: A direct revelation: 'And the land of Egypt shall be a desolation and a waste. Then they will know that I am YHWH — because he said, The Nile is mine and I made it.' The judgment's purpose is revelation: yad'u ki-ani YHWH, they shall know that I am YHWH. The fundamental sin judged is not military alliance but the claim to self-creation. In Christ, the Creator becomes a creature (John 1:14) — not to abdicate his creative authority but to restore what the creature's rebellion had broken. The recognition formula finds its fullest expression in Philippians 2:10-11: every knee shall bow and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord.
10: A structural note: 'I am against you and against your streams and I will make the land of Egypt an utter waste and desolation from Migdol to Syene, as far as the border of Cush.' Migdol (northeastern border) to Syene (modern Aswan, southern boundary) designates all of Egypt. The scope of judgment matches the scope of Pharaoh's claims: he claimed the whole Nile, so the whole land is judged. This geographic totality points toward the equally universal scope of Christ's final judgment: “all nations shall be gathered before him” (Matthew 25:32).
11: A structural note: 'No foot of man shall pass through it and no foot of beast shall pass through it; it shall be uninhabited forty years.' The forty-year desolation echoes Israel's forty years in the wilderness — the period of purification and transformation. The number forty consistently signals testing and transition (Moses on Sinai, Israel in the desert, Jesus in the wilderness). Egypt will undergo the same wilderness-period it imposed on Israel. What Egypt inflicted, it now experiences — the lex talionis of divine history.
12: A structural note: 'And I will scatter the Egyptians among the nations and disperse them through the countries.' Egypt scattered among the nations is the reverse of Israel's exile to Egypt. In Genesis, Jacob's family went down to Egypt for refuge; now Egypt is scattered as Israel had been. The dispersal-judgment that fell on Israel for covenant infidelity falls on Egypt for its anti-covenantal role. Every nation that is used to oppress YHWH's people faces the same scattering that his people experienced.
13: A pattern: 'For thus says the Lord GOD: At the end of forty years I will gather the Egyptians from the peoples among whom they were scattered.' The gathering after scattering is the pattern of divine restoration running throughout Ezekiel (Ezekiel 36–37). After judgment comes the ingathering. This pattern points toward the final gathering that Christ enacts: “the Son of Man will send his angels and they will gather his elect from the four winds” (Matthew 24:31). Even Egypt — the paradigmatic oppressor — is gathered and restored, demonstrating that YHWH's purpose is always ultimately restorative, not merely punitive.
14: A pattern: 'And I will restore the fortunes of Egypt and bring them back to the land of Pathros, the land of their origin, and there they shall be a lowly kingdom.' Egypt restored to Pathros and made a lowly kingdom: the proud empire humbled to its beginnings. The pattern of humiliation-and-restoration is the crucifixion-resurrection shape of divine history. Egypt goes from empire to lowly kingdom — from Pharaoh's self-declared greatness to a modest, chastened state. What the cross does to human pride is what YHWH does here to Egypt: it is not destroyed but reconfigured.
15: A pattern: 'It shall be the most lowly of the kingdoms and never again exalt itself above the nations.' Never again will Egypt exalt itself — the proud empire is permanently humbled. This is the beatitude pattern of Christ's kingdom: “the meek shall inherit the earth” (Matthew 5:5); “whoever exalts himself will be humbled and whoever humbles himself will be exalted” (Matthew 23:12). The humbling of Egypt is not revenge but the reordering of all things under YHWH's kingdom — preparation for the world in which the lowly are the great.
16: A direct revelation: 'And it shall never again be the confidence of the house of Israel, recalling their iniquity when they turn to them for aid. Then they will know that I am YHWH.' YHWH removes Egypt as a false confidence precisely so that Israel will look to him alone. Every broken prop — every failed alliance, every collapsed human institution — is YHWH's invitation to find confidence in him. Paul's “such is the confidence that we have through Christ toward God” (2 Corinthians 3:4) is the NT voice of a people for whom Egypt has finally been removed as an option.
17: A structural note: 'In the twenty-seventh year in the first month on the first day of the month the word of YHWH came to me.' This date — 571 BCE — is the latest of all Ezekiel's dated oracles, seventeen years after the first Egypt oracle (Ezekiel 29:1). The oracles against Egypt span Ezekiel's entire prophetic ministry, suggesting that Egypt is not a peripheral concern but a structural feature of his vision: the great world-power that embodied opposition to YHWH's purposes must be comprehensively judged before the new creation can emerge.
18: A structural note: 'Son of man, Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon made his army labor hard against Tyre. Every head was made bald and every shoulder was rubbed bare, yet neither he nor his army got anything from Tyre as wages for the labor he had performed against it.' Nebuchadnezzar's unsuccessful siege of Tyre left his army with nothing. YHWH's compensation: Egypt will be given as wages. This divine economy — where human instruments of judgment receive divine compensation — shows YHWH's sovereign management of history. Christ as the one “through whom and for whom all things were created” (Colossians 1:16) is the one who assigns historical outcomes.
19: A pattern: 'Therefore thus says the Lord GOD: Behold I will give the land of Egypt to Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon; and he shall carry off its wealth and despoil it and plunder it; and it shall be the wages for his army.' Nebuchadnezzar as YHWH's instrument of judgment against Egypt demonstrates that YHWH's sovereignty extends over all nations, not just Israel. The NT expands this: Pilate has no authority except what is given from above (John 19:11); even the Roman empire that crucified Jesus was an instrument used within YHWH's purposes, and the cross — the apparent defeat — was the decisive victory.
20: A pattern: 'I have given him the land of Egypt as his payment for which he labored, because they worked for me, declares the Lord GOD.' Nebuchadnezzar “worked for me” — the foreign king, unknowing, served YHWH's purposes. This is the pattern of the Joseph narrative (Genesis 50:20: you intended evil, but God intended it for good) and of the cross: those who crucified Christ were doing what God's hand had predestined to take place (Acts 4:28), and the act intended for evil became the source of universal salvation.
21: A direct revelation: 'On that day I will cause a horn to spring up for the house of Israel, and I will open your lips among them. Then they will know that I am YHWH.' The “horn” (qeren) is the standard OT symbol for messianic power (Psalms 132:17: “there I will make a horn to spring up for David”). Zechariah's Benedictus draws on exactly this language: “He has raised up a horn of salvation for us in the house of his servant David” (Luke 1:69). The oracle against Egypt closes with a messianic note: Israel's salvation is the positive purpose behind Egypt's judgment. Egypt's judgment and the Messiah's horn are the two sides of YHWH's eschatological program.
